Services
Tax-Efficient Strategies Minimize taxes with smart withdrawal sequencing. Portfolio Management Diversified portfolios aligned with your timeline. Retirement Money Map A clear plan for every dollar in retirement. Social Security Optimization Time your benefits to maximize lifetime income. Estate & Legacy Planning Protect your family and pass wealth efficiently.

Client Login Schedule a Visit

CHAPTER I: SOCIAL SECURITY FUNDAMENTALS

The Federal Advantage in Social Security

Federal employees have unique Social Security considerations — the FERS supplement, coordination with your pension, and elimination of WEP/GPO penalties as of January 1, 2025. Strategic claiming can add thousands to your lifetime benefits.

Optimize My Social Security

"FERS employees have an advantage — WEP and GPO are history, and you have the supplement to bridge the gap."

— Mike Stevens, IAR

On This Page

  • FERS Supplement
  • WEP/GPO Eliminated
  • Claiming Strategy
  • FERS Coordination
  • Spousal Strategy
  • Tax Optimization
CHAPTER II: THE BRIDGE

FERS Supplement: Your Bridge to 62

The FERS supplement is federal employees' secret weapon — a special payment that bridges the income gap from retirement until Social Security eligibility at age 62. This benefit can provide $1,200-$2,000 monthly, but it comes with strict eligibility rules and earnings limitations.

FERS Supplement Eligibility

Retirement Type Age Requirement Service Requirement Supplement Eligible?
MRA + 30 years Your MRA (56-57) 30 years Yes - Full supplement
Age 60 + 20 years 60 20 years Yes - Full supplement
Age 62 + 5 years 62 5 years No - Can claim actual SS
MRA + 10 years Your MRA (56-57) 10-29 years No - Reduced pension
$1,800 Average Monthly Supplement
62 Age When Supplement Ends
$23,400 2025 Earnings Test Limit

FERS Supplement Earnings Test

If you receive the FERS supplement and work after retirement, your supplement is reduced $1 for every $2 you earn over $23,400 (2025 limit). This makes post-retirement employment planning crucial for supplement recipients. The test only applies to earned income — investment income, TSP withdrawals, and your FERS annuity don't count.

The supplement automatically ends when you turn 62, regardless of whether you claim Social Security. This creates a potential income cliff that must be planned for — your supplement might stop, but you may choose to delay actual Social Security for higher benefits later.

CHAPTER III: GOOD NEWS

WEP and GPO Eliminated

Effective January 1, 2025, the Windfall Elimination Provision (WEP) and Government Pension Offset (GPO) were permanently eliminated. This is huge news for federal employees who were previously affected by these complex and often unfair reductions to Social Security benefits.

What This Means for Federal Employees

  • WEP Elimination: If you had previous non-covered government employment, your Social Security benefits are no longer reduced
  • GPO Elimination: Spousal and survivor Social Security benefits are no longer offset by your federal pension
  • Full Benefits: You receive your complete Social Security benefit based on your earnings record
  • Retroactive Relief: Current retirees affected by WEP/GPO received retroactive payments and benefit adjustments

Before vs. After WEP/GPO Elimination

Before (Pre-2025)

  • × Social Security benefits reduced by up to $512/month (WEP)
  • × Spousal benefits reduced by 2/3 of government pension (GPO)
  • × Complex calculations and exceptions
  • × Many affected retirees unaware of impact

After (2025+)

  • ✓ Full Social Security benefits based on earnings record
  • ✓ Full spousal and survivor benefits available
  • ✓ Simplified planning and calculations
  • ✓ Significantly higher retirement income for many

This change is particularly beneficial for federal employees with mixed careers — those who worked in the private sector, state/local government, or had military service before federal employment. Previously complex calculations are now straightforward: you get your full Social Security benefit based on your complete earnings history.

Current federal retirees who were receiving reduced benefits received automatic adjustments and retroactive payments. If you retired before 2025 and were affected by WEP or GPO, your benefits were automatically corrected.

CHAPTER IV: TIMING

Optimal Claiming Strategy

Federal employees have unique flexibility in Social Security timing because you already have guaranteed pension income from FERS. This allows you to potentially delay Social Security for higher benefits without relying solely on investments for income.

62
Earliest Age

Permanently reduced benefits. May make sense if you need income or have health concerns.

75% of Full Benefit
66-67
Full Retirement Age

Full benefit amount based on your earnings record. No reduction or credit.

100% of Full Benefit
70
Maximum Age

Maximum benefit with delayed retirement credits. Often optimal for federal retirees.

132% of Full Benefit

Federal Employee Claiming Considerations

Reasons to Claim Early (62)

  • Need the income immediately
  • Health concerns affect longevity
  • Want to start benefits while FERS supplement still active
  • Spouse optimization strategies

Reasons to Delay to 70

  • FERS annuity provides sufficient income
  • Good health and family longevity
  • Want maximum inflation-protected benefit
  • Tax diversification with TSP withdrawals

The 8% annual increase for delaying Social Security from Full Retirement Age to 70 is a guaranteed, inflation-adjusted return that's hard to beat. For federal employees with secure FERS income, this delay often makes financial sense if health and longevity support it.

Key insight: Unlike private sector workers who might need Social Security income immediately upon retirement, federal employees can often afford to optimize timing because they have predictable FERS annuity income throughout retirement.

CHAPTER V: INTEGRATION

FERS and Social Security Coordination

FERS was designed as a three-legged stool: FERS annuity, Social Security, and TSP. Understanding how these pieces work together is essential for optimizing your total retirement income and minimizing taxes.

The Federal Retirement Income Stack

FERS Annuity

Guaranteed monthly income starting at retirement. Includes annual COLAs for inflation protection.

Immediate Income

Social Security

Inflation-adjusted income starting at 62-70. Timing choice affects lifetime benefits significantly.

Optimizable Timing

TSP Withdrawals

Flexible withdrawals to fill income gaps and provide tax diversification options.

Flexible Supplement

Strategic coordination involves timing Social Security to maximize lifetime benefits while using TSP withdrawals to bridge any income gaps. This approach allows you to optimize each income stream individually while maintaining your desired lifestyle throughout retirement.

40% FERS Annuity of Total Income
30% Social Security of Total Income
30% TSP of Total Income

Both FERS annuity and Social Security are fully taxable as ordinary income (though Social Security may be partially taxable depending on your total income). Strategic TSP withdrawals can help manage your tax bracket and avoid triggering higher taxes on Social Security benefits.

CHAPTER VI: SPOUSAL COORDINATION

Married Couples Strategy

Married federal employees have additional Social Security strategies available, including spousal benefits and survivor planning. With WEP/GPO eliminated, these strategies are now straightforward and potentially very valuable.

Spousal Social Security Strategies

Strategy When to Use Key Benefit Considerations
Spousal Benefits Lower-earning spouse Up to 50% of higher earner's benefit Must wait until higher earner claims
Survivor Benefits When one spouse dies 100% of deceased spouse's benefit Critical for FERS + SS coordination
Claiming Coordination Different optimal ages Maximize household lifetime benefits May involve one early, one delayed

With GPO eliminated, federal employee spouses can now receive full spousal and survivor Social Security benefits without reduction. This significantly improves retirement security for federal families, especially when combined with FERS survivor annuity elections.

Survivor planning is particularly important for federal couples because the surviving spouse loses the FERS annuity (unless survivor benefits were elected) but can inherit the higher of the two Social Security benefits. This affects both Social Security claiming timing and FERS survivor benefit elections.

CHAPTER VII: TAX OPTIMIZATION

Social Security Tax Planning

Social Security benefits can be taxable depending on your total income, and federal employees with multiple income sources (FERS, Social Security, TSP) need strategic planning to minimize taxes throughout retirement.

Social Security Taxation Thresholds

Filing Status Income Range Taxable SS Percentage Federal Tax Strategy
Single Under $25,000 0% Very low income scenario
Single $25,000 - $34,000 50% Moderate FERS + TSP withdrawals
Single Over $34,000 85% Most federal retirees with FERS
Married Filing Jointly Under $32,000 0% Very low income scenario
Married Filing Jointly $32,000 - $44,000 50% Strategic income management zone
Married Filing Jointly Over $44,000 85% Most federal couples with FERS

Most federal retirees will have 85% of their Social Security benefits taxable due to their FERS annuity income. However, strategic TSP withdrawal management can help optimize total tax burden and potentially qualify for lower capital gains rates on investment income.

Medicare IRMAA Considerations

High-income federal retirees may face Medicare Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amounts (IRMAA) surcharges on Medicare premiums. Strategic timing of Social Security, TSP withdrawals, and Roth conversions can help manage modified adjusted gross income to avoid or minimize these surcharges.

Advanced strategy: Federal employees with both traditional and Roth TSP balances have opportunities for tax optimization. By coordinating Social Security timing with strategic Roth TSP withdrawals (tax-free) and traditional TSP withdrawals (taxable), you can potentially manage your tax bracket throughout retirement.

The elimination of WEP and GPO makes Social Security planning much simpler for federal employees — you get your full benefit, and you can focus on optimizing timing and tax management rather than navigating complex reduction formulas.

CHAPTER VIII: COMMON QUESTIONS

Social Security FAQ

Everything you need to know about Social Security as a federal employee.

Optimize My Strategy

The FERS supplement is a special payment designed to bridge the income gap between when you retire and when you become eligible for Social Security at age 62. It approximates the Social Security benefit you earned during your FERS-covered employment. You only qualify for the supplement if you retire at your Minimum Retirement Age (MRA) with 30 years of service, or at age 60 with 20 years of service. The supplement is subject to an earnings test similar to Social Security — if you have substantial earned income from working, your supplement may be reduced or eliminated. The supplement automatically ends when you turn 62, regardless of whether you actually claim Social Security at that time.

The Windfall Elimination Provision (WEP) can reduce Social Security benefits for workers who receive a pension from employment not covered by Social Security. For FERS employees, this primarily applies if you have previous employment where you didn't pay Social Security taxes (such as certain state/local government jobs or foreign employment), or if you have time under the old Civil Service Retirement System (CSRS). Pure FERS employees who have always paid Social Security taxes throughout their careers are generally NOT affected by WEP. If you do have non-covered employment, the WEP reduction depends on how many years of substantial earnings you have under Social Security — having 30 or more years of substantial earnings eliminates the WEP reduction entirely.

The optimal claiming age depends on your individual situation. You can claim as early as 62 (with a permanent reduction), wait until your Full Retirement Age (66-67 depending on birth year) for your full benefit, or delay until age 70 for an 8% annual increase. Because FERS employees already have a guaranteed pension (FERS annuity), you may have more flexibility than private sector workers. Delaying Social Security can make sense if you're healthy and expect longevity, especially since the higher benefit is inflation-adjusted for life. We model your specific situation — including your FERS annuity amount, TSP balance, health, and life expectancy — to determine which claiming strategy maximizes your lifetime income.

FERS was designed as a three-part retirement system: FERS annuity, Social Security, and TSP. Your FERS annuity begins when you retire and continues for life. Social Security can start anytime from age 62 to 70, and also continues for life with annual cost-of-living adjustments. Together, these provide a foundation of guaranteed lifetime income. You then supplement with TSP withdrawals as needed. Both your FERS annuity and Social Security are taxable income (though the portion of Social Security that's taxable depends on your total income). Strategic planning involves coordinating the timing of Social Security, managing TSP withdrawals tax-efficiently, and ensuring your combined income supports your lifestyle without pushing you into higher tax brackets or triggering Medicare IRMAA surcharges.

The Government Pension Offset (GPO) can reduce Social Security spousal or survivor benefits if you receive a pension from government employment not covered by Social Security. Similar to WEP, this primarily affects employees with CSRS service or other non-covered government employment. FERS employees who have always paid Social Security taxes are generally not subject to GPO. If GPO does apply, it can reduce spousal or survivor benefits by two-thirds of your government pension amount. Understanding whether GPO affects your situation is important for couples planning survivor income and coordinating spousal benefits.

gap: 1px; background: var(--outline-variant); border-radius: var(--radius-lg); overflow: hidden; } .related-post-card { background: white; padding: 2rem; text-decoration: none; transition: all 0.2s ease; position: relative; border-bottom: 3px solid transparent; } .related-post-card:hover { border-bottom-color: var(--secondary); transform: translateY(-2px); } .related-post-date { font-family: var(--font-mono); font-size: 0.75rem; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.05em; color: var(--on-surface-variant); margin-bottom: 0.75rem; } .related-post-title { font-family: var(--font-body); font-size: 1.25rem; font-weight: 600; line-height: 1.4; color: var(--on-surface); margin-bottom: 0.75rem; } .related-post-link { font-family: var(--font-body); font-size: 0.875rem; font-weight: 500; color: var(--secondary); text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.05em; } /* Navy CTA Section */ .service-cta { background: var(--primary); padding: 4rem 0; text-align: center; color: white; } .service-cta-inner { max-width: 600px; margin: 0 auto; padding: 0 2rem; } .service-cta h2 { font-family: var(--font-serif-display); font-style: italic; font-size: 2.5rem; font-weight: 400; margin-bottom: 1rem; } .service-cta p { font-family: var(--font-body); font-size: 1.125rem; line-height: 1.6; margin-bottom: 2rem; opacity: 0.9; } .service-cta-button { display: inline-flex; align-items: center; gap: 0.5rem; font-family: var(--font-body); font-weight: 600; font-size: 1rem; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.05em; padding: 1rem 2rem; background: white; color: var(--primary); border-radius: 16px; text-decoration: none; transition: all 0.2s ease; } .service-cta-button:hover { background: var(--surface-container-low); transform: translateY(-2px); box-shadow: 0 8px 20px rgba(0,0,0,0.15); } /* Responsive Design */ @media (max-width: 768px) { .service-header { padding: 2rem 0 3rem; } .service-title { font-size: 2.5rem; } .service-subtitle { font-size: 1.125rem; } .related-posts-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } .related-posts { padding: 3rem 0 4rem; } .service-cta { padding: 3rem 0; } .service-cta h2 { font-size: 2rem; } }

SOCIAL SECURITY FOR FEDS

Federal Social Security Coordination

Federal employees face unique Social Security considerations including the FERS supplement and coordination with your pension. We help optimize your claiming strategy.

CHAPTER I: SOCIAL SECURITY FUNDAMENTALS

The Federal Advantage in Social Security

Federal employees have unique Social Security considerations — the FERS supplement, coordination with your pension, and elimination of WEP/GPO penalties as of January 1, 2025. Strategic claiming can add thousands to your lifetime benefits.

Optimize My Social Security

"FERS employees have an advantage — WEP and GPO are history, and you have the supplement to bridge the gap."

— Mike Stevens, IAR

On This Page

  • FERS Supplement
  • WEP/GPO Eliminated
  • Claiming Strategy
  • FERS Coordination
  • Spousal Strategy
  • Tax Optimization
CHAPTER II: THE BRIDGE

FERS Supplement: Your Bridge to 62

The FERS supplement is federal employees' secret weapon — a special payment that bridges the income gap from retirement until Social Security eligibility at age 62. This benefit can provide $1,200-$2,000 monthly, but it comes with strict eligibility rules and earnings limitations.

FERS Supplement Eligibility

Retirement Type Age Requirement Service Requirement Supplement Eligible?
MRA + 30 years Your MRA (56-57) 30 years Yes - Full supplement
Age 60 + 20 years 60 20 years Yes - Full supplement
Age 62 + 5 years 62 5 years No - Can claim actual SS
MRA + 10 years Your MRA (56-57) 10-29 years No - Reduced pension
$1,800 Average Monthly Supplement
62 Age When Supplement Ends
$23,400 2025 Earnings Test Limit

FERS Supplement Earnings Test

If you receive the FERS supplement and work after retirement, your supplement is reduced $1 for every $2 you earn over $23,400 (2025 limit). This makes post-retirement employment planning crucial for supplement recipients. The test only applies to earned income — investment income, TSP withdrawals, and your FERS annuity don't count.

The supplement automatically ends when you turn 62, regardless of whether you claim Social Security. This creates a potential income cliff that must be planned for — your supplement might stop, but you may choose to delay actual Social Security for higher benefits later.

CHAPTER III: GOOD NEWS

WEP and GPO Eliminated

Effective January 1, 2025, the Windfall Elimination Provision (WEP) and Government Pension Offset (GPO) were permanently eliminated. This is huge news for federal employees who were previously affected by these complex and often unfair reductions to Social Security benefits.

What This Means for Federal Employees

  • WEP Elimination: If you had previous non-covered government employment, your Social Security benefits are no longer reduced
  • GPO Elimination: Spousal and survivor Social Security benefits are no longer offset by your federal pension
  • Full Benefits: You receive your complete Social Security benefit based on your earnings record
  • Retroactive Relief: Current retirees affected by WEP/GPO received retroactive payments and benefit adjustments

Before vs. After WEP/GPO Elimination

Before (Pre-2025)

  • × Social Security benefits reduced by up to $512/month (WEP)
  • × Spousal benefits reduced by 2/3 of government pension (GPO)
  • × Complex calculations and exceptions
  • × Many affected retirees unaware of impact

After (2025+)

  • ✓ Full Social Security benefits based on earnings record
  • ✓ Full spousal and survivor benefits available
  • ✓ Simplified planning and calculations
  • ✓ Significantly higher retirement income for many

This change is particularly beneficial for federal employees with mixed careers — those who worked in the private sector, state/local government, or had military service before federal employment. Previously complex calculations are now straightforward: you get your full Social Security benefit based on your complete earnings history.

Current federal retirees who were receiving reduced benefits received automatic adjustments and retroactive payments. If you retired before 2025 and were affected by WEP or GPO, your benefits were automatically corrected.

CHAPTER IV: TIMING

Optimal Claiming Strategy

Federal employees have unique flexibility in Social Security timing because you already have guaranteed pension income from FERS. This allows you to potentially delay Social Security for higher benefits without relying solely on investments for income.

62
Earliest Age

Permanently reduced benefits. May make sense if you need income or have health concerns.

75% of Full Benefit
66-67
Full Retirement Age

Full benefit amount based on your earnings record. No reduction or credit.

100% of Full Benefit
70
Maximum Age

Maximum benefit with delayed retirement credits. Often optimal for federal retirees.

132% of Full Benefit

Federal Employee Claiming Considerations

Reasons to Claim Early (62)

  • Need the income immediately
  • Health concerns affect longevity
  • Want to start benefits while FERS supplement still active
  • Spouse optimization strategies

Reasons to Delay to 70

  • FERS annuity provides sufficient income
  • Good health and family longevity
  • Want maximum inflation-protected benefit
  • Tax diversification with TSP withdrawals

The 8% annual increase for delaying Social Security from Full Retirement Age to 70 is a guaranteed, inflation-adjusted return that's hard to beat. For federal employees with secure FERS income, this delay often makes financial sense if health and longevity support it.

Key insight: Unlike private sector workers who might need Social Security income immediately upon retirement, federal employees can often afford to optimize timing because they have predictable FERS annuity income throughout retirement.

CHAPTER V: INTEGRATION

FERS and Social Security Coordination

FERS was designed as a three-legged stool: FERS annuity, Social Security, and TSP. Understanding how these pieces work together is essential for optimizing your total retirement income and minimizing taxes.

The Federal Retirement Income Stack

FERS Annuity

Guaranteed monthly income starting at retirement. Includes annual COLAs for inflation protection.

Immediate Income

Social Security

Inflation-adjusted income starting at 62-70. Timing choice affects lifetime benefits significantly.

Optimizable Timing

TSP Withdrawals

Flexible withdrawals to fill income gaps and provide tax diversification options.

Flexible Supplement

Strategic coordination involves timing Social Security to maximize lifetime benefits while using TSP withdrawals to bridge any income gaps. This approach allows you to optimize each income stream individually while maintaining your desired lifestyle throughout retirement.

40% FERS Annuity of Total Income
30% Social Security of Total Income
30% TSP of Total Income

Both FERS annuity and Social Security are fully taxable as ordinary income (though Social Security may be partially taxable depending on your total income). Strategic TSP withdrawals can help manage your tax bracket and avoid triggering higher taxes on Social Security benefits.

CHAPTER VI: SPOUSAL COORDINATION

Married Couples Strategy

Married federal employees have additional Social Security strategies available, including spousal benefits and survivor planning. With WEP/GPO eliminated, these strategies are now straightforward and potentially very valuable.

Spousal Social Security Strategies

Strategy When to Use Key Benefit Considerations
Spousal Benefits Lower-earning spouse Up to 50% of higher earner's benefit Must wait until higher earner claims
Survivor Benefits When one spouse dies 100% of deceased spouse's benefit Critical for FERS + SS coordination
Claiming Coordination Different optimal ages Maximize household lifetime benefits May involve one early, one delayed

With GPO eliminated, federal employee spouses can now receive full spousal and survivor Social Security benefits without reduction. This significantly improves retirement security for federal families, especially when combined with FERS survivor annuity elections.

Survivor planning is particularly important for federal couples because the surviving spouse loses the FERS annuity (unless survivor benefits were elected) but can inherit the higher of the two Social Security benefits. This affects both Social Security claiming timing and FERS survivor benefit elections.

CHAPTER VII: TAX OPTIMIZATION

Social Security Tax Planning

Social Security benefits can be taxable depending on your total income, and federal employees with multiple income sources (FERS, Social Security, TSP) need strategic planning to minimize taxes throughout retirement.

Social Security Taxation Thresholds

Filing Status Income Range Taxable SS Percentage Federal Tax Strategy
Single Under $25,000 0% Very low income scenario
Single $25,000 - $34,000 50% Moderate FERS + TSP withdrawals
Single Over $34,000 85% Most federal retirees with FERS
Married Filing Jointly Under $32,000 0% Very low income scenario
Married Filing Jointly $32,000 - $44,000 50% Strategic income management zone
Married Filing Jointly Over $44,000 85% Most federal couples with FERS

Most federal retirees will have 85% of their Social Security benefits taxable due to their FERS annuity income. However, strategic TSP withdrawal management can help optimize total tax burden and potentially qualify for lower capital gains rates on investment income.

Medicare IRMAA Considerations

High-income federal retirees may face Medicare Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amounts (IRMAA) surcharges on Medicare premiums. Strategic timing of Social Security, TSP withdrawals, and Roth conversions can help manage modified adjusted gross income to avoid or minimize these surcharges.

Advanced strategy: Federal employees with both traditional and Roth TSP balances have opportunities for tax optimization. By coordinating Social Security timing with strategic Roth TSP withdrawals (tax-free) and traditional TSP withdrawals (taxable), you can potentially manage your tax bracket throughout retirement.

The elimination of WEP and GPO makes Social Security planning much simpler for federal employees — you get your full benefit, and you can focus on optimizing timing and tax management rather than navigating complex reduction formulas.

CHAPTER VIII: COMMON QUESTIONS

Social Security FAQ

Everything you need to know about Social Security as a federal employee.

Optimize My Strategy

The FERS supplement is a special payment designed to bridge the income gap between when you retire and when you become eligible for Social Security at age 62. It approximates the Social Security benefit you earned during your FERS-covered employment. You only qualify for the supplement if you retire at your Minimum Retirement Age (MRA) with 30 years of service, or at age 60 with 20 years of service. The supplement is subject to an earnings test similar to Social Security — if you have substantial earned income from working, your supplement may be reduced or eliminated. The supplement automatically ends when you turn 62, regardless of whether you actually claim Social Security at that time.

The Windfall Elimination Provision (WEP) can reduce Social Security benefits for workers who receive a pension from employment not covered by Social Security. For FERS employees, this primarily applies if you have previous employment where you didn't pay Social Security taxes (such as certain state/local government jobs or foreign employment), or if you have time under the old Civil Service Retirement System (CSRS). Pure FERS employees who have always paid Social Security taxes throughout their careers are generally NOT affected by WEP. If you do have non-covered employment, the WEP reduction depends on how many years of substantial earnings you have under Social Security — having 30 or more years of substantial earnings eliminates the WEP reduction entirely.

The optimal claiming age depends on your individual situation. You can claim as early as 62 (with a permanent reduction), wait until your Full Retirement Age (66-67 depending on birth year) for your full benefit, or delay until age 70 for an 8% annual increase. Because FERS employees already have a guaranteed pension (FERS annuity), you may have more flexibility than private sector workers. Delaying Social Security can make sense if you're healthy and expect longevity, especially since the higher benefit is inflation-adjusted for life. We model your specific situation — including your FERS annuity amount, TSP balance, health, and life expectancy — to determine which claiming strategy maximizes your lifetime income.

FERS was designed as a three-part retirement system: FERS annuity, Social Security, and TSP. Your FERS annuity begins when you retire and continues for life. Social Security can start anytime from age 62 to 70, and also continues for life with annual cost-of-living adjustments. Together, these provide a foundation of guaranteed lifetime income. You then supplement with TSP withdrawals as needed. Both your FERS annuity and Social Security are taxable income (though the portion of Social Security that's taxable depends on your total income). Strategic planning involves coordinating the timing of Social Security, managing TSP withdrawals tax-efficiently, and ensuring your combined income supports your lifestyle without pushing you into higher tax brackets or triggering Medicare IRMAA surcharges.

The Government Pension Offset (GPO) can reduce Social Security spousal or survivor benefits if you receive a pension from government employment not covered by Social Security. Similar to WEP, this primarily affects employees with CSRS service or other non-covered government employment. FERS employees who have always paid Social Security taxes are generally not subject to GPO. If GPO does apply, it can reduce spousal or survivor benefits by two-thirds of your government pension amount. Understanding whether GPO affects your situation is important for couples planning survivor income and coordinating spousal benefits.
